template_description: Social-ecological system template for marine pollution, covering chemical and nutrient contamination, habitat degradation, human welfare impacts, and environmental regulation responses. Applicable to estuarine and coastal systems.

connections.description: Salmon and sea trout fishery demand affects Salmon stocking and hatchery operations

Expanding port capacity requires deepening and maintaining navigation channels. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
Dredging operations directly resuspend contaminated sediments and increase turbidity

Ongoing discharges add to legacy contamination in estuarine sediments. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
Dams and weirs block upstream migration, preventing salmon from reaching spawning grounds

Excessive nutrients fuel algal growth; decomposition consumes oxygen in stratified estuary waters. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation
Atlantic salmon migration success affects Salmon fishery productivity

Productive salmon runs attract recreational anglers and generate local tourism income. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally

Removing dams or installing fish ladders directly eliminates migration barriers. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms
Modern treatment removes heavy metals from effluent, reducing ongoing contamination
Channelisation removes natural substrate and flow variability, reducing benthic habitat quality
Healthy benthic communities provide prey for juvenile fish in estuarine nurseries. Bottom trawling physically disrupts benthic habitat structure, removing biogenic features.
Growing angler demand for salmon and sea trout drives recreational fishing participation and effort in Baltic estuaries
High nutrient loading changes water density and stratification, interacting with flow regulation to alter the estuarine salinity gradient
Bottom-water oxygen levels directly control benthic invertebrate survival and community composition in stratified estuaries
Nursery function for coastal fish species affects Recreational fishing tourism revenue
River basin nutrient management plans impose discharge limits that reduce industrial and municipal wastewater nutrient loads
Flood defence construction has only indirect effects on nutrient enrichment through altered hydrology. The link is tenuous. Strength reduced from medium to weak.
Sand and gravel extraction may resuspend some nutrients from sediments but is not a significant source of nutrient enrichment compared to agricultural/wastewater sources. Strength reduced to weak.

Salmon stock recovery plans (HELCOM) focus on fish passage restoration, stocking, and fishing regulation, not nutrient reduction. Only indirect link through broader ecosystem management. Strength and confidence reduced.

Contaminated sediment remediation targets heavy metals and organic pollutants, not nutrient enrichment. May even temporarily increase nutrient release during disturbance. Strength and confidence reduced.
Industrial pre-treatment reduces pollutant load entering wastewater treatment plants
Green infrastructure implements river basin nutrient management plan objectives
Recreational fishing tourism revenue reinforces Recreational boating and waterfront access
Flood damage avoidance for riverside communities reinforces Flood risk management needs
Drinking water quality for downstream users reinforces Industrial water supply demand
Port and navigation accessibility reinforces Port and harbour expansion pressure
